summary: 88q exfiltrates console output to attacker-controlled Telegram bot on require
details: On require('88q'), index.js runs an IIFE that globally overrides console.warn and console.error. The replacement functions take their first argument and POST it via fetch() to https://api.telegram.org/bot<redacted-token>/sendMessage with hardcoded chat_ids -1001161709623 and -1001433099398 (index.js:7, 12-22). Any downstream code in the installer's process that writes to console.warn/console.error — application error handlers, logging libraries, framework warnings — silently leaks those arguments to a third-party Telegram channel for the life of the process. The package exports nothing useful (module.exports is undefined from an IIFE with no return), confirming the only purpose is the side-effect global hijack. This is a one-way exfiltration channel from installer process to attacker infrastructure and meets the active-attack threshold.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
